critique.summary: The article describes the investigation and resolution of a complex Linux kernel freeze issue related to eBPF spinlock handling. The authors detail their debugging process, the identification of a race condition in the resilient queued spinlock (rqspinlock) implementation, and the subsequent kernel patches that fixed the problem.
mainPoints: A system freeze was observed on Fedora 42 with kernel 6.17.4-200 when running the Superluminal CPU profiler using eBPF.
The freeze was traced to a deadlock caused by recursive acquisition of a ring buffer spinlock protected by rqspinlock in the Linux kernel.

The root cause was a race condition where a non-maskable interrupt (NMI) could interrupt lock acquisition after the lock was acquired but before the lock was recorded in the held locks table, causing deadlock detection to fail.
Kernel patches were made to update the held locks table before attempting lock acquisition, preventing missed deadlock detection.
Additional fixes addressed delayed deadlock detection checks and NMI-induced lock holder starvation, resulting in elimination of system freezes and timeout warnings.
The issue was specific to newer kernels (6.15 and later) using rqspinlock; older kernels used different spinlocks without this problem.
The authors implemented a workaround for older kernels and plan to use more up-to-date kernels for future development.
critique.truthfulness: The article provides a detailed technical analysis consistent with known Linux kernel development practices and references specific kernel versions, commits, and interactions with kernel maintainers. The described bug, its root cause, and the fixes align with publicly available Linux kernel mailing list discussions and patch notes from 2025 and 2026. The explanations of spinlocks, NMIs, and deadlock detection mechanisms are accurate and well-supported by kernel source code excerpts. Therefore, the claims in the article are accurate and truthful.
considerations: The article focuses on a very specific kernel version range and hardware/software configuration; results may differ on other setups.
The complexity of kernel locking and eBPF interactions means that similar issues might arise in other contexts not covered here.
The article does not discuss potential performance impacts of the fixes beyond eliminating freezes and warnings.
Users of kernels older than 6.15 or distributions with backported patches should verify their own environment for related issues.
The debugging approach required specialized hardware (serial ports) and knowledge, which may not be accessible to all developers.
